reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Comprehensive coverage of every CDN configuration area from the source types — caching, forwarding, route rewrites, edge functions, custom domains, price class, firewall, cache invalidation, and default documents are all addressed with grounded content.
Every H2 section includes a working, realistic code example with correct focus markers and TypeScript label — reader can copy and ship without guessing.
Strong decision support throughout: each opt-in section explains when to enable it, when to skip it, and what the tradeoff is (cost, complexity, edge cases).

The route rewrites origin-type table (bucket, http-api-gateway, application-load-balancer, function, custom-origin) and the edge functions hook-point table are correctly derived from source types and give the reader exactly the lookup they need.
Warning callout about the Authorization header requiring `cacheKeyParameters` forwarding (not just `forwardingOptions`) is source-grounded and practically important — a common gotcha.
Warning about `onOriginRequest` being used internally by Stacktape matches the JSDoc exactly and prevents readers from breaking their own CDN setup.

H2 ordering and noun-form heading style match the example page exactly. Shared sections (When to use, When NOT to use, Basic example, Custom domains, Referenceable parameters, API Reference, FAQ) appear in the same relative order.
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and choosing/comparison questions.
Opening paragraph is self-contained, 40–60 words, and includes a pricing line sourced from the JSDoc — matches example page's opening rhythm.
Tip and Warning callouts are used only where the source justifies them, matching the example page's MDX component palette for those callout types.
problems: MDX component palette drift: the example page uses `<ReferenceableParams resource="http-api-gateway" />` in its referenceable parameters section; this CDN page replaces it with a hand-authored markdown table. This is a palette drift violation per the review rules — penalize scannability by 1.

The 'Default documents' section mixes two logically separate concerns: `indexDocument`/`errorDocument` (only meaningful for bucket-backed CDN distributions) and `defaultRoutePrefix` (applies to all origin types). The prose does not clarify which properties are bucket-specific vs universal, which could mislead readers attaching CDN to an API gateway.

The basic example shows an HTTP API Gateway as the parent resource. For CDN documentation, a bucket-backed SPA is arguably the most common use case (and the one with meaningful default caching behavior). Using an API gateway obscures the caching defaults that differ by origin type.

The intro pricing line ('~$0.01 per 10,000 requests') and the FAQ pricing line ('~$0.0075–$0.016 per 10,000 requests') are inconsistent — the intro gives a flat figure while the FAQ correctly uses a range. Both are within what the source supports but the difference can confuse readers who read both.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the hand-authored markdown table in 'Referenceable parameters' with `<ReferenceableParams resource="cdn" />` to match the MDX component palette used in the example page. If 'cdn' is not a valid resource identifier for the component (since CDN is a sub-property rather than a top-level resource type), investigate the correct resource string — but do not silently fall back to a manual table without confirming the component cannot support this page. Palette drift rule applies regardless of workaround.
optionalImprovements: Clarify in 'Default documents' that `indexDocument` and `errorDocument` apply specifically to bucket-backed CDN distributions, not to API Gateway or ALB origins — the prose currently implies they are universal CDN settings.

Consider showing a bucket-backed CDN example in 'Basic example' (or as a second code block) since static site/SPA hosting is the most common reason teams add CDN. The API Gateway example is valid but the bucket origin shows the meaningful default caching behavior difference upfront.

Unify the pricing representation: use the same format (range or approximate) in both the opening paragraph and the FAQ answer about CloudFront cost. '~$0.01/10k' in the intro and '$0.0075–$0.016/10k' in the FAQ are both source-defensible but create a perception mismatch.

The 'Forwarding' section's `allowedMethods` prose does not explicitly state the default ('all methods' per the JSDoc). Adding a one-line clarification ('By default, all methods are forwarded to the origin') would remove ambiguity for readers who want to know what they get without configuring this property.

The 'Cache invalidation' section is thin — it describes the toggle (`disableInvalidationAfterDeploy`) and a tip about content-hashed filenames, but doesn't mention that CloudFront propagates invalidations globally over roughly 60 seconds. Adding that timing note would complete the mental model readers need for production deploys.

Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.

Consistent noun-form H2s, ApiReference at the bottom, Tip/Warning callout usage, and frontmatter shape all match the example page's conventions.

Every opt-in section (caching, route rewrites, forwarding, edge functions, firewall, price class, cache invalidation) provides explicit when-to-enable and when-to-skip guidance — this is the correct guide-not-recipe treatment.
The route rewrites section covers all five origin types (bucket, http-api-gateway, application-load-balancer, function, custom-origin) with a clear table, matching the union variants in the source.
The Authorization header caveat in the Forwarding section is a genuine production gotcha that is correctly sourced from the JSDoc and surfaced prominently with a Warning callout.
The onOriginRequest warning accurately reflects the source JSDoc ('Stacktape uses this trigger internally') and prevents a real misconfiguration.
FAQ questions span all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and comparison/choosing questions.
problems: All code examples use HttpApiGateway as the parent resource, including the 'Default documents' section (indexDocument, errorDocument) — but those properties are only meaningful for bucket-backed CDN distributions. Showing these settings on an HttpApiGateway example is misleading and produces a confusing experience for the most common CDN use case (static site / SPA hosting from a bucket).

The page never shows a Bucket-backed CDN example, despite bucket being the primary CDN use case (described first in 'When to use' as static sites and SPAs). Users deploying a static site must mentally translate every example from HttpApiGateway to Bucket.

Referenceable parameters are documented via a hand-authored markdown table instead of the <ReferenceableParams /> component the example page uses. While CDN is a sub-property and resource="cdn" may not work, the deviation from the example's MDX component palette should be noted and evaluated.

The cache key section says 'for headers: none or whitelist' without mentioning that ForwardHeaders has allViewer, allViewerAndWhitelistCloudFront, and allExcept options — these are separate from the cache key (CacheKeyHeaders which genuinely is only none/whitelist), but the prose conflates cache-key options with forwarding options in a way that may confuse readers.

The 'Default documents' H2 section blurs two distinct concepts: indexDocument/errorDocument (which only apply to bucket origins) and defaultRoutePrefix (which applies to all origins). Mixing these under one section title is confusing since they have different scopes.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the 'Default documents' section's HttpApiGateway code example with a Bucket example that actually demonstrates indexDocument and errorDocument in a meaningful context, since these properties serve no purpose on an API gateway origin. Consider splitting defaultRoutePrefix into a separate paragraph or note rather than grouping it with bucket-only document defaults.

Add at least one Bucket-backed CDN code example early in the page (in or immediately after the Basic example section). The opening paragraph and 'When to use' section both lead with static sites as the primary use case, but every example uses HttpApiGateway. This gap will actively confuse the majority of CDN users.
optionalImprovements: Consider using <ReferenceableParams resource="http-api-gateway" /> (or a note explaining that CDN params appear in the parent resource's referenceable params) rather than a hand-authored table — this would match the example page's component palette and keep CDN param documentation in sync with schema changes automatically.

The 'Cache key' subsection accurately states that for CacheKeyHeaders the options are none or whitelist. Consider adding a one-sentence callout that more forwarding strategies (allViewer, allExcept, allViewerAndWhitelistCloudFront) exist at the forwarding level but not at the cache-key level, to prevent confusion between the two related-but-distinct concepts.

The Basic example section mentions 'Buckets get additional CDN options like rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp for SPA hosting' — this property is not visible in the provided source files (cdn.d.ts). Verify it exists in the Bucket type before keeping this claim, or remove it to avoid an unsupported assertion.
The 'Forwarding' section's code block has a trailing newline before the closing backtick, making it visually inconsistent with the other code blocks on the page (minor cosmetic issue).

The FAQ answer for 'How fast does CloudFront propagate changes?' says 'typically complete within 60 seconds' for invalidations — this is an AWS-side claim that is accurate as a general AWS fact but could be softened to 'usually within 60 seconds, though global propagation can take a few minutes' to acknowledge real-world variance without overstating precision.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is exactly the right length (~70 words), self-contained, and answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — including pricing context from source in one sentence.
All 5 route rewrite origin types (bucket, http-api-gateway, application-load-balancer, function, custom-origin) are enumerated in a scannable table, satisfying union-type coverage discipline.
Decision support is present in every opt-in section (When to enable / When to skip with concrete tradeoff signals) — Caching, Firewall, Price class, Cache invalidation all follow this pattern.
Code examples are complete, realistic, and copy-paste ready — focus markers are consistent, imports are hidden correctly, no placeholder values.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions mixing Stacktape-specific ('What happens to the CDN cache when I deploy?'), AWS use-case ('How fast does CloudFront propagate changes?'), and choosing/comparison questions ('When should I add a CDN to my API?').
Cache key section clearly distinguishes include-in-cache-key from forward-to-origin behavior, and the Tip callout ('keep the cache key as narrow as possible') is actionable and opinionated.
The Warning about Authorization header forwarding requiring cacheKeyParameters is source-grounded and would prevent a real production bug.

Noun-form H2s throughout, consistent with the example page heading style. H2 ordering follows the example (When to use → When NOT to use → Basic example → resource-specific sections → Referenceable parameters → API Reference → FAQ).
problems: Component palette drift (mandatory): The 'Referenceable parameters' section uses a hand-written markdown table instead of the `<ReferenceableParams resource='cdn' />` component that the example page uses. This is an explicit MDX component palette drift violation — the example uses `<ReferenceableParams>` and this page does not.

For FAQ answers about well-known AWS facts this is permissible per the instructions, but reviewers may flag it as an invented number — the source-grounded figure ('~$0.01 per 10,000 requests') is safer.

The 'Referenceable parameters' section opens with 'CDN parameters are accessed through the parent resource name using `$ResourceParam()`' — this is a correct and useful clarification, but describing `cdnCustomDomains` as 'Comma-separated list' is an inference not explicitly stated in the source type definition.
mandatoryFixes: Component palette drift — Replace the hand-written markdown table in the 'Referenceable parameters' section with `<ReferenceableParams resource='cdn' />` to match the example page's pattern. The current table is accurate but using the component ensures consistency across all pages in the section and is a required match per the example-guided mode rules.

Instead, tighten the section to cover only `indexDocument` and `errorDocument`, then add a cross-link to Route rewrites for the prefix behavior.

issues.statement: The ApiReference uses `definitionName="CdnConfiguration"` which is the base type, but bucket-backed CDN uses `BucketCdnConfiguration` (adds `rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp`, `disableUrlNormalization`) and ALB-backed CDN uses `ApplicationLoadBalancerCdnConfiguration` (adds `listenerPort`, `originDomainName`). The API reference will not show these resource-specific properties.
issues.reason: The page mentions these extended properties in prose (e.g., 'Buckets get additional CDN options like rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp') but the ApiReference component pointing at the base CdnConfiguration will not expose them. Readers looking up these properties in the reference will not find them.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a note near the ApiReference that bucket-attached CDN and ALB-attached CDN expose additional properties beyond the base configuration, and link to the respective resource pages (/resources/storage/s3-bucket, /resources/networking/application-load-balancer) for those extended properties.

issues.statement: The Firewall section introduces the opt-in feature but lacks cost/tradeoff signal. It says 'When to enable' and 'When to skip' but doesn't mention WAF pricing (per-rule, per-request charges) which is important for the reader's decision.
issues.reason: The documentation-quality rules require opt-in feature sections to include a cost/tradeoff signal. WAF adds per-web-ACL and per-rule monthly fees plus per-request charges — this is a significant cost factor that affects the reader's decision to enable it.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a brief cost context sentence, e.g., 'AWS WAF charges a monthly fee per web ACL and per rule, plus per-request inspection charges — budget for this in production.' This is general AWS knowledge and doesn't require strict source grounding.

evidence.quote: useFirewall?: string;

issues.statement: The Custom domains section doesn't mention that CloudFront custom domain certificates must be in the us-east-1 region, which is a well-known AWS requirement that catches users off guard.
issues.reason: CloudFront requires ACM certificates to be in us-east-1 regardless of the origin's region. This is a common pitfall when users provide a customCertificateArn from another region. Since the page documents the customCertificateArn option, this caveat is important for readers using that feature.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a note: 'If you provide a `customCertificateArn`, the ACM certificate must be in the `us-east-1` region — this is an AWS CloudFront requirement regardless of where your origin resources are deployed.'

evidence.quote: customCertificateArn?: string;

issues.statement: The Edge functions section warns that 'Stacktape uses onOriginRequest internally for bucket, web service, and API Gateway CDN setups' but the Warning component does not mention that onOriginResponse may also have internal usage implications.
issues.reason: The source JSDoc for onOriginRequest explicitly warns about internal usage. The onOriginResponse JSDoc says 'Modify the response before it's cached and returned. Changes are cached as if they came from the origin.' — no internal usage warning. The draft's warning accurately reflects the source, so this is a minor improvement suggestion rather than an error.
issues.suggestedFix: No change needed — the warning correctly reflects the source. This is informational only.

evidence.quote: Warning: Stacktape uses this trigger internally for bucket/web-service/API Gateway CDN setups. Overriding it may break default behavior. Only use if you know what you're doing.

issues.statement: The Default documents section says 'For bucket-backed CDN distributions, the indexDocument and errorDocument properties control which files are served' but these properties exist on the base CdnConfiguration interface, available to all CDN-attachable resources, not just buckets.
issues.reason: The source shows indexDocument and errorDocument on CdnConfiguration (the base interface), not on BucketCdnConfiguration. While these properties likely only have practical effect for bucket origins, the draft's framing as 'bucket-backed CDN distributions' implies they don't apply to other resource types at the type level.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften the framing: 'The indexDocument and errorDocument properties are most relevant for bucket-backed CDN distributions, where they control which files are served for root requests and 404 errors.'

verifierResults.summary: The CDN draft page is factually well-grounded. All Stacktape-specific claims about CDN properties, defaults, supported resource types, route rewrite origins, edge function hooks, price classes, and referenceable parameters match the source types exactly. No invented properties or incorrect defaults were found. The five issues raised are medium-to-low severity: (1) the ApiReference pointing at the base CdnConfiguration won't surface bucket-specific or ALB-specific extended properties, (2) the Firewall opt-in section lacks cost/tradeoff context, (3) the custom domains section should note the us-east-1 certificate requirement, (4-5) minor framing improvements. No high-severity factual errors were identified.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided CDN types, but several claims overstate what the source supports or introduce AWS/Stacktape behavior not present in the source. The most important fixes are to narrow WebSocket/HTTP claims, remove or qualify unsupported custom-domain and propagation details, fix the route rewrite example property names, and avoid claiming ALB top-level CDN options that only appear on route rewrite origins.

issues.statement: In the route rewrite example, the draft creates `const assets = new Bucket({})` but routes to `properties: { bucketName: 'assets' }`.
issues.reason: The source defines `bucketName` as the name of the `bucket` resource. The example's resource name is `assets`, but the string value is manually written and could be a valid name. This becomes a concrete bug only if the code-block validator expects the property to reference the resource key; still, the example is fragile because readers may think the string is arbitrary. Use the same name explicitly and explain it, or name the variable/resource `assetsBucket` and use `bucketName: 'assetsBucket'`.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the returned resource key and route target string identical, for example `return { resources: { assetsBucket, myApi } }` and `bucketName: 'assetsBucket'`.

issues.statement: “ALBs get optional `listenerPort` and `originDomainName` properties for custom listener setups.”
issues.reason: The provided CDN source only shows `listenerPort` and `originDomainName` inside `CdnLoadBalancerOrigin`, which is the `routeTo` shape for route rewrites. The draft presents them as additional CDN options for ALBs generally, which could mislead readers into looking for these properties on top-level `cdn` configuration.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement to route rewrites: “When a route rewrite targets an Application Load Balancer, the `application-load-balancer` origin can include `listenerPort` and `originDomainName` for custom listener or origin-domain setups.”

issues.statement: “WebSocket or streaming connections — CloudFront handles HTTP request-response only.”
issues.reason: The CDN source does not state that Stacktape CDN is HTTP-only or unsupported for WebSocket/streaming. The claim is also too broad as general AWS wording. Because this section tells readers not to use the feature, it should be grounded or narrowed.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a narrower, source-grounded caution such as: “For traffic that should not be cached or transformed at the edge, use the direct Application Load Balancer or API Gateway endpoint unless you specifically need CloudFront behavior.”

issues.statement: “Stacktape can attach your own domain… Stacktape automatically provisions a free TLS certificate and creates a DNS record in Route53.” and “Each domain gets its own certificate, managed and auto-renewed by AWS.”
issues.reason: The source supports automatic DNS records and TLS certificates, and supports `customCertificateArn` and `disableDnsRecordCreation`. It does not support the draft's stronger claim that each domain gets its own certificate or that certificates are auto-renewed by AWS.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the supported behavior: “Stacktape automatically creates DNS records and provisions a free TLS certificate unless you provide `customCertificateArn` or disable DNS record creation.” Remove “each domain gets its own certificate” and “auto-renewed” unless another source provides it.

issues.statement: “The firewall protects the CDN from common web exploits, provides rate limiting, IP filtering, geo-blocking, and bot protection.”
issues.reason: The CDN source only says `useFirewall` names a `web-app-firewall` resource to protect the CDN from common web exploits. The specific capabilities listed may be true for WAF generally, but they are not present in the provided source files for this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Limit this page to the grounded attachment behavior and link to the WAF page: “`useFirewall` names a `web-app-firewall` resource that protects the CDN from common web exploits. Configure rules on the firewall resource itself.”

issues.statement: “CloudFront cache invalidations typically complete within 60 seconds… New CloudFront distributions can take 5–15 minutes to deploy…”
issues.reason: These timing numbers are not present in the provided source files. They are general AWS operational guidance, but the draft presents them as concrete timing expectations. Since the page requirements say numbers should come from source unless general FAQ knowledge is clearly allowed, these exact timings should be removed or softened.
issues.suggestedFix: Use non-numeric wording unless a source is provided: “Cache invalidations and distribution changes are asynchronous and can take time to propagate globally.”

issues.statement: “For bucket origins, responses are cached for 6 months and invalidated automatically on each deploy.”
issues.reason: The source says bucket origins are cached for 6 months when the origin response has no `Cache-Control` header. The draft says this correctly in one bullet, but the later sentence omits the “when no Cache-Control header is present” condition and may overstate the default.
issues.suggestedFix: Add the condition wherever the statement is repeated: “For bucket origins with no `Cache-Control` header, responses are cached for 6 months and invalidated automatically on deploy.”

issues.statement: “If you use content-hashed filenames… disabling automatic invalidation is safe.”
issues.reason: The source supports disabling invalidation when you manage cache invalidation yourself or want cached content between deploys, but it does not specifically guarantee safety for content-hashed filenames. This is reasonable guidance, but the wording is absolute.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften to: “If all cacheable assets use content-hashed filenames and HTML is handled separately, disabling automatic invalidation is usually a good fit.”

The page correctly identifies CDN as a `cdn` property attached to supported resources rather than a standalone Stacktape resource.
The draft correctly documents the supported CDN route rewrite origin variants from the source: bucket, HTTP API Gateway, Application Load Balancer, Lambda function, and custom origin.
The referenceable parameter table matches the `CdnReferenceableParam` union in the source.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Comprehensive union-type coverage: all five `routeTo` origin types documented in a table; all cookie/header/query-string forwarding variants (none, whitelist, allViewer, allViewerAndWhitelistCloudFront, allExcept, allExcept) named explicitly in prose.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Comprehensive, source-grounded coverage of all CDN configuration options — caching, forwarding, route rewrites, edge functions, price class, firewall, and custom domains all get working examples and decision guidance.
Strong use of tables for structured comparisons: price classes, route origin types, edge function hook timings, and referenceable parameters make the right option easy to find.

Well-placed warnings on operationally important gotchas: the Authorization header forwarding requirement, the onOriginRequest internal-use caveat, and the ACM us-east-1 constraint are exactly what a reader needs before hitting production.

Decision support throughout — each opt-in feature section covers when to enable it, when the default is fine, and a cost or tradeoff signal. The Firewall and Price class sections are particularly well-structured.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, and comparison), and answers are appropriately concise and citation-friendly.
Code examples are realistic and copyable with property-value anchors: every non-obvious value in examples (PriceClass_100, defaultTTL, cacheMethods) gets a prose explanation.
problems: The 'Document defaults' section is three sentences with no code example and no guidance on when a reader would change these values from the defaults. It reads as a thin config reference fragment rather than actionable guidance. The API reference already documents the same defaults.

Missing operational gotcha: new CloudFront distributions and major config changes (price class, custom domains) propagate asynchronously and can take time before fully effective. This is directly relevant to dev workflow and deployment expectations, but the page only mentions it in the context of cache invalidation in FAQ answer 8, not proactively where readers need it (e.g., basic example section or When NOT to use).

Lambda function CDN attachment: the basic example section and intro claim CDN can attach to a Lambda function, but the route rewrite `CdnLambdaFunctionOrigin` source specifically notes the function must have `url.enabled: true`. It is not clear from the page whether this constraint also applies when CDN is the top-level attachment to a Lambda function rather than a rewrite target — this ambiguity could cause a runtime failure for readers.
optionalImprovements: Expand or absorb the 'Document defaults' section: either add a short code example showing how to customize `indexDocument` and `errorDocument` and explain when you would (e.g., 404.html override for React SPAs, custom error pages), or merge the content into the bucket CDN basic example where it is directly relevant and remove the standalone section.

Add a propagation-time note in the basic example or the When NOT to use section: new CloudFront distributions and major config changes can take time to propagate globally. Readers iterating on CDN configuration during development will hit this and may be confused about why changes are not taking effect.

Clarify the Lambda function `url.enabled: true` constraint: the route rewrite docs are explicit about the requirement, but the top-level CDN-on-Lambda scenario is not addressed. Either confirm the same constraint applies and add a note in the basic example, or clarify it does not apply at the top level. Ambiguity here causes a hard-to-debug deployment failure.

Consider moving `<ApiReference definitionName="CdnConfiguration" />` to just above the FAQ: because CDN is a sub-property rather than a standalone resource, some readers arrive to look up a specific property name. Positioning the API reference near the end of prose content (before FAQ) gives those readers a clear 'look it up here' anchor without interrupting the tutorial flow.

The Forwarding section's list of header forwarding strategies (none, whitelist, allViewer, allViewerAndWhitelistCloudFront, allExcept) appears in dense prose. A small table — mode, effect, when to use — would make this easier to scan, matching the treatment given to price classes and route types.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Comprehensive union-type coverage: all five `routeTo` origin types documented in a table; all cookie/header/query-string forwarding variants (none, whitelist, allViewer, allViewerAndWhitelistCloudFront, allExcept, allExcept) named explicitly in prose.
Tables used where they matter most: price class tradeoffs, route origin types, edge function hook timing, referenceable parameters — all tabularized, making citation easy.

Decision support is explicit for every opt-in feature: Firewall has 'When to enable / When to skip' bullets; price class has an opinionated Tip; cache invalidation explains the default and the tradeoff for disabling.

All six CDN referenceable parameters from the source (`cdnDomain`, `cdnUrl`, `cdnCustomDomains`, `cdnCustomDomainUrls`, `cdnCanonicalDomain`, `cdnCanonicalUrl`) are tabularized and match the source type exactly.

Warning callouts correctly flag the two important gotchas sourced directly from the type JSDoc: `Authorization` header must go through cache-key parameters, and `onOriginRequest` is used internally by Stacktape.
10-question FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and choosing/comparison), including pricing, propagation behavior, and WAF tradeoffs.
Examples are realistic and copy-paste ready: consistent resource names, no `<fill-in-here>` placeholders, imports visible in source but hidden behind focus markers.
No marketing language, no hollow claims — language is concrete, pragmatic, and opinionated throughout.
Answer-first paragraph is well within the 40-75 word target and self-contained, suitable for AI extraction.

Route rewrites section clearly differentiates `routeTo` property names (`bucketName`, `httpApiGatewayName`, etc.) as Stacktape resource keys, not AWS physical names — a real gotcha explained correctly.
problems: The 'Document defaults' section (indexDocument / errorDocument) is effectively two sentences with no code example and no decision-support context. It reads like a thin reference entry rather than a guide section. For an audience trying to decide whether to configure these, there is no signal about when the defaults are wrong and no example showing how to set them.

The Basic example section mentions `$ResourceParam('myApi', 'cdnUrl')` and `stackConfig.outputs` in a subordinate aside, but does not show the wiring. A reader seeing this for the first time has no path to complete the task — this should either be shown inline or deferred explicitly to a cross-link.

The 'When to use' section's bullet list is pattern-based but has no concrete latency or cost signal. 'Lower latency' without an order-of-magnitude reference is vague for a reader deciding whether CDN is worth the complexity for their use case.

The cache key section's cookie mode `allExcept` is mentioned in the source type (`CacheKeyCookies.allExcept`) but not named in the prose listing of cookie options ('The options are `none`, `all`, `whitelist`, or `allExcept`'). The prose does mention it but only as part of the cache key description — the forwarding section's cookie options ('the supported modes are `none`, `whitelist`, and `all`') is incomplete because `ForwardCookies` does not include `allExcept`, which is correct, but the asymmetry between caching cookie options and forwarding cookie options is never explained.
optionalImprovements: Expand the 'Document defaults' section: add a one-paragraph explanation of when the defaults are insufficient (multi-page apps vs SPAs, custom 404 pages), and add a short code example showing both `indexDocument` and `errorDocument` set together. Currently the section is too thin to justify its H2 status.

In the Referenceable parameters section, show a concrete `stackConfig.outputs` snippet using `$ResourceParam('myApi', 'cdnUrl')` so readers see the end-to-end wiring rather than an inline reference with no example.

Add one concrete latency signal to the 'When to use' section — for example, 'users in Asia or South America hitting a US-East origin typically see 100–300 ms round-trip savings from the nearest CloudFront edge' — to give readers a quantitative anchor for the CDN decision.

In the 'When to use' bullets, the 'edge authentication' bullet mentions edge functions but does not link to the edge function docs. Add a parenthetical link to `/resources/compute/edge-function` at first mention.

The forwarding section's `Authorization` header Warning is important but positioned mid-section. Consider elevating it to an `<Info>` callout near the top of the Forwarding section, since it catches a large class of auth bugs early.

The 'Cache key' H3 explains the options via prose enumeration. Adding a small table (cookie modes: none/whitelist/allExcept/all; header modes: none/whitelist; query-string modes: all/none/whitelist) would make it more scannable and consistent with the style used in other sections.

The 'Route rewrites' code example uses `routeTo.properties.bucketName: 'assetsBucket'` where `assetsBucket` is the Stacktape resource key. The prose explains this correctly, but placing this clarification before the code block rather than after would reduce confusion on first read.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly well-grounded in the provided CDN type sources. I found one material overstatement around edge functions that should be narrowed so readers do not assume every edge hook avoids the origin.

issues.statement: “Edge functions run custom code at CloudFront edge locations on each CDN request or response, without hitting your origin.”
issues.reason: Only `onRequest` is documented as able to return before hitting the origin. `onOriginRequest` runs before forwarding to the origin after a cache miss, and `onOriginResponse` runs after the origin responds, so the blanket “without hitting your origin” claim is wrong for several hook points.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the opening to: “Edge functions run custom code at CloudFront hook points on CDN requests and responses. `onRequest` can return early before the origin is contacted; origin hooks run around cache misses and origin responses.”

evidence.quote: Use to modify the request, add auth checks, or return an immediate response without hitting the origin.
Only runs on cache misses. Use to modify the request before it reaches your origin.
Name of an `edge-lambda-function` to run after the origin responds (before caching).

positiveFindings: All Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and avoid hand-authored YAML.
The supported CDN parent resources match `StpCdnAttachableResourceType`: bucket, application-load-balancer, http-api-gateway, and function.

Caching defaults, price classes, forwarding options, route rewrite origin variants, custom domain behavior, cache invalidation, and referenceable parameter names are consistent with the provided CDN type source.
